title: Metasurfaces for neutral-atom trapping
authors: Chengyu Fang, Minjeong Kim, Mark Saffman, Jennifer T. Choy, Mikhail Kats
abstract: Trapped neutral atoms are one of the leading platforms for quantum information technologies, in particular for quantum computing, but scaling them to array sizes needed for utility-scale quantum computing is a major engineering challenge. Here we review optical metasurfaces as an enabling technology that provides fine control over the phase, amplitude, and polarization of light, with pixel counts far exceeding what is available with spatial light modulators (SLMs) and other active devices. The large pixel counts have recently led to demonstrations of arrays of optical tweezers with hundreds of thousands of sites and arrays of optical bottle-beams with complex three-dimensional trapping profiles. The flexibility and scalability of optical metasurfaces provides a route towards miniaturized, integrated, and highly scalable atomic experiments and instruments.
summary: This review argues that optical metasurfaces are crucial for advancing neutral-atom quantum technologies by overcoming the scaling limitations of existing optical devices. They enable the creation of massive, highly controllable arrays of optical traps, such as bottle-beams, which are necessary for utility-scale quantum computing. The integration of metasurfaces promises to miniaturize and simplify complex atomic trapping setups.
main_problem: Scaling neutral-atom arrays to the millions of sites required for utility-scale quantum computing presents a major engineering challenge.
main_result: Optical metasurfaces are presented as a highly scalable enabling technology that surpasses the pixel limitations of active devices like SLMs for generating complex, large-scale optical trap arrays.
method: The authors review and propose using metasurfaces to generate optical tweezers and complex trapping profiles, comparing this approach against traditional active and passive optical components.
model_or_system: The system involves trapping neutral atoms (e.g., for quantum computing) using light fields generated by optical metasurfaces. These metasurfaces provide subwavelength control over the phase, amplitude, and polarization of light.
key_observables: Trap site count (scaling to hundreds of thousands or millions), phase/amplitude/polarization control, and diffraction efficiency.
important_parameters: Pixel count (bottleneck for SLMs), trap array size (millions of sites), and operational bandwidth.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that metasurfaces can effectively replace multiple complex optical components, enabling miniaturization and high scalability.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison of scaling capabilities between SLMs and metasurfaces, and demonstrate the generation of complex 3D trapping profiles like optical bottle-beams using metasurfaces.
paper_structure: The paper structure moves from identifying the scaling bottleneck in current trapping methods (SLMs, MLAs) to proposing metasurfaces as the superior solution, detailing their capabilities for complex beam shaping and integration.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the hardware limitations for building large quantum simulators or quantum computers based on neutral atoms, bridging the gap between fundamental quantum physics and practical quantum engineering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Approaches to scale neutral-atom tweezer arrays. (a) Schematic of a neutral-atom quan- tum computer, where atoms are trapped in an optical-tweezer array, with qubits encoded in two electronic states (|0⟩and |1⟩). A Rydberg state (|r⟩) enables entanglement between neighboring atoms, enabling multi-qubit gates such as the CNOT shown on the right. (b) An array of 12,000 tweezer sites generated by two 1024 × 1024 SLMs (∼2 million pixels in total) using 1061 nm and 1055 nm laser, with 6,100 atoms loaded; adapted from [5]. (c) An array of 78,400 tweezer sites gen- erated by a single 5-mm-diameter metasurface with 106 million pixels (no atoms loaded); adapted from [27]. (d) An array of...

Figure 2: Generation of optical bottle beams. (a) Bottle-beam trap generated by destructive interference of two co-propagating Gaussian beams with different waists, stabilized by an active phase lock; adapted from [56]. (b) Single dark trap generated from an incident Gaussian beam by a ∼1-mm metasurface, optimized for high efficiency and trap quality; adapted from [57]. (c) Polarization-multiplexed metasurface that generates a bright trap for one circular polarization and a dark trap for the opposite circular polarization; adapted from [58]. (d) One-dimensional array of ten dark traps generated by a metasurface; five of the sites are shown in X-Z and Y-Z, for the optic axis along Z; adapted...

Figure 3: Multi-functionality, miniaturization, and integration enabled by metasurfaces in atom- trapping optical systems. (a) Multi-functional metasurface for generating a MOT, combining beam splitting and polarization control to generate five cooling beams from one incident beam; adapted from [64]. (b) Polarization-multiplexed metalens, operating for two wavelengths (780 nm and 850 nm) for two orthogonal linear polarizations; adapted from [38]. (c) Wavelength-multiplexed metalens at 780 nm and 852 nm, which adds quarter-wave-plate functionality only at 852 nm; adapted from [66]. (d–g) Representative compact metasurfaces that range from sub-millimeter to a few millimeters; adapted from...

Figure 4: Optical materials for neutral-atom trapping metasurfaces (a) Refractive index (n) as a function of wavelength for metasurface materials, including a-Si [85], c-Si [29], TiO2 [79], Si3N4 [86]. Solid curves indicate low-loss regions with κ < 0.01, while dashed curves indicate absorbing regions with a larger extinction coefficient. Vertical dashed lines mark several representative atomic transitions: Yb at 399 nm, Sr at 461 nm, Rb D2 at 780 nm, Cs D2 at 852 nm. (b) Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of metasurfaces fabricated from a-Si [65], c-Si [29], TiO2 [59], SiNx [64].
